haasini_manne.name: Haasini Manne
haasini_manne.summary:
Out of Texas, Haasini Manne is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 4 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Haasini is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
haasini_manne.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Haasini Manne, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Haasini is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Frisco South ORC, and so far, the only thing Haasini has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Haasini saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Haasini plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 4 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Haasini has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Haasini showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U15, which means Haasini traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 DFW South OLC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Haasini entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Haasini paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Haasini is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Haasini wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Haasini, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

haasini_vidapalapati.name: Haasini Vidapalapati
haasini_vidapalapati.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Haasini Vidapalapati has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 6 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Haasini competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Haasini has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

hailey_hung.name: Hailey Hung
hailey_hung.summary:
Out of California, Hailey Hung has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-4 in XD U17. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Hailey belongs with the best in the country.

Hailey is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals top-4 (XD U17) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Hailey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Jerry Yuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Arthur Heng Lee — a trusted U19/XD partner (4 events, best: #3)

Hailey has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Hailey has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Hailey brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
hailey_hung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hailey Hung, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 28 career entries in 13 tournaments, Hailey has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 27 entries where Hailey drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 28 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Hailey saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Hailey would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Hailey has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Hailey has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Hailey's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hailey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 28 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Hailey has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Jerry Yuan: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jerry Yuan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in GD U19 — we hope Brianna Mia Xu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Hailey entered 6 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Hailey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Hailey and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Hailey 3 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hailey is not just losing locally — Hailey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hung family travels cross-country so Hailey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hailey has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hailey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U19, which means Hailey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 28 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Hailey. Hailey just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Hailey has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Hailey. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Hailey: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

hailey_lam.name: Hailey Lam
hailey_lam.summary:
A California competitor, Hailey Lam is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the final four 6 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Notably, Hailey has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Hailey especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Hailey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Aaron MX Tsai (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Crystal Sun (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Angie Lu — 2 events in GD, best: #4

Hailey has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Hailey's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
hailey_lam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hailey Lam, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Hailey's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Hailey keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Hailey saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Hailey would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Hailey has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Hailey has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Hailey's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hailey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Hailey has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13 — we hope Luke Ai has emotionally recovered.

Hailey has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hailey has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hailey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U13, which means Hailey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Hailey entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Hailey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hailey keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hailey has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hailey, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hailey. We will be here, taking notes.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Hamilton saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 1 entries and a best of 65th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hamilton plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Hamilton has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Hamilton has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hamilton has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hamilton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Hamilton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Hamilton entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Hamilton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 14 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Hamilton. Hamilton just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Hamilton has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Hamilton. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Hamilton: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

hanlan_hu.name: Hanlan Hu
hanlan_hu.summary:
Out of Maryland, Hanlan Hu is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U15 level, with 10 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Notably, Hanlan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Hanlan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Hanlan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Hanlan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
hanlan_hu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hanlan Hu, from Maryland, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Hanlan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Hanlan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Hanlan saga began at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hanlan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Hanlan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BD U15 — we hope Ivan Jia has emotionally recovered.

Hanlan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hanlan has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hanlan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U15, which means Hanlan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Hanlan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Hanlan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hanlan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hanlan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hanlan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hanlan. We will be here, taking notes.

hannah_cheng.name: Hannah Cheng
hannah_cheng.summary:
Out of New York, Hannah Cheng knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 36 entries across 15 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Hannah has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Hannah competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Hannah has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Edward Chang (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Haoran Xia — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Hannah is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Hannah is still ascending.

Hannah has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Hannah isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Hannah is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
hannah_cheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hannah Cheng, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 36 entries in 15 tournaments. That is a 3% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Hannah shows up to a tournament, Hannah pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Hannah saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2026 Wayside NE CRC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Hannah's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Hannah would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 7 times Hannah has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 7 times Hannah has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Hannah's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hannah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Hannah is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Hannah and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Hannah needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Hannah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hannah's texts. The go-to partner is Haoran Xia (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Haoran Xia has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Hannah's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Hannah has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Hannah has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hannah has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hannah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Hannah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Hannah entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Hannah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 36 entries, and 35 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hannah looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hannah's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hannah has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hannah. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

hannah_fang.roast:

Hannah is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Hannah's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Hannah is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Hannah is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Hannah wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Hannah, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

  • Kanyanat Vajworarat — 5 events, 1 title together in GD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Suneri Krishna Chinthalapati — a trusted GD/U17 partner (6 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Hannah has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Hannah has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Hannah consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 6 titles, Hannah has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
hannah_george.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hannah George, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 66 career entries in 24 tournaments, Hannah has 6 titles — a 9% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 10 entries where Hannah drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 66 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Hannah saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Hannah's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Hannah actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Hannah is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Hannah experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Hannah has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, where Hannah entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Hannah does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Hannah has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 6 wins means Hannah is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hannah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Hannah can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Hannah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hannah's texts. The go-to partner is Ronith A Manikonda (19 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Ronith A Manikonda has emotionally recovered.

Of Hannah's 6 career titles, 5 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Hannah feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Hannah entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Hannah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Hannah and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Hannah 8 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Tournament committees have handed Hannah a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Hannah has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U17, where Hannah was seeded #1 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Hannah has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hannah is not just losing locally — Hannah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The George family travels cross-country so Hannah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hannah has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hannah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Hannah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Hannah entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Hannah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 66 entries, and 60 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hannah looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hannah's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hannah has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hannah. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

hannah_xiao.name: Hannah Xiao
hannah_xiao.summary:
Representing California, Hannah Xiao stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U13 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Hannah has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Hannah especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • 1 ORC title.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Hannah adapts to anyone:
  • Eli You (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Qingyao Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Hannah is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Hannah is still ascending.

Hannah has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Hannah has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Hannah consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Hannah's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
hannah_xiao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hannah Xiao, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 25 career entries in 9 tournaments, Hannah has 1 title — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 24 entries where Hannah drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 25 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Hannah saga began at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Hannah's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hannah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Hannah is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Hannah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hannah's texts. The go-to partner is Eli You (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jason Zhu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Hannah entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Hannah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Hannah and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Hannah has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Hannah has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hannah is not just losing locally — Hannah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xiao family travels cross-country so Hannah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hannah has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hannah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13, which means Hannah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Hannah entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Hannah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 25 entries, and 24 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hannah looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hannah's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hannah has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hannah. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

hannya_keswani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hannya Keswani, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Hannya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Hannya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Hannya saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hannya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Hannya has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Varna Prakash (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jimmy Qinmo Fan has emotionally recovered.

Hannya has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hannya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hannya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Hannya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Hannya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Hannya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hannya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hannya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hannya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hannya. We will be here, taking notes.

hansika_malladi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hansika - Malladi, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-4. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Hansika has 42 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Hansika saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Hansika's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 12 entries — a stretch where Hansika actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Hansika is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Hansika experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Hansika has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NW CRC, where Hansika entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U15, XD U15, GD U15). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Hansika does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Hansika has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 1 wins means Hansika is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hansika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Hansika is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Hansika and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Hansika needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Hansika has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hansika's texts. The go-to partner is Sizhe Chen (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Xinyang Peng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Hansika's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. Junior Nationals has seen Hansika 6 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Hansika has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hansika is not just losing locally — Hansika is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Malladi family travels cross-country so Hansika can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hansika has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hansika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Hansika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Hansika entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Hansika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 42 entries, and 41 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hansika looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hansika's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hansika has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hansika. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Hanson is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Hanson wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Hanson, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

hanwen_xiong.name: Hanwen Xiong
hanwen_xiong.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Hanwen Xiong is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U13 level, with 6 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Hanwen competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Hanwen has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
hanwen_xiong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hanwen Xiong, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Hanwen is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 NE MassBad OLC, and so far, the only thing Hanwen has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Hanwen saga began at 2025 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hanwen has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hanwen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U11, which means Hanwen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Hanwen entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Hanwen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hanwen keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hanwen has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hanwen, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hanwen. We will be here, taking notes.

hanwen_yuan.name: Hanwen Yuan
hanwen_yuan.summary:
Out of Washington, Hanwen Yuan knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 31 entries across 11 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Hanwen is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Hanwen adapts to anyone:
  • Yiyang Jia (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Avery Ji (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Having moved up to U17, Hanwen is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Hanwen brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Hanwen has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Hanwen has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Hanwen's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
hanwen_yuan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hanwen Yuan, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Hanwen has 31 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Hanwen saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Hanwen's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where Hanwen actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Hanwen is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hanwen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Hanwen is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Hanwen and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Hanwen needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Hanwen has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hanwen's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Yiyang Jia: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Yiyang Jia has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Leheng Xu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Hanwen's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Hanwen has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hanwen is not just losing locally — Hanwen is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yuan family travels cross-country so Hanwen can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hanwen has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hanwen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Hanwen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Hanwen entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Hanwen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 31 entries, and 30 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hanwen looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hanwen's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hanwen has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hanwen. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

hanyi_sissi_lei.name: Hanyi (Sissi) Lei
hanyi_sissi_lei.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Hanyi (Sissi) Lei is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GD U13) puts Hanyi in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Hanyi has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Hanyi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U13) • 1 ORC title.

Diving into the match data reveals: Hanyi boasts a solid 64% win rate over 76 matches, 45 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a biggest win margin of 37 points.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Hanyi adapts to anyone:
  • Daisy Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Steven Lei (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Kaylie Liu (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD
  • Jacob Ma (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Hanyi is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Hanyi has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Hanyi has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 12 seeded entries across the career, Hanyi consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Hanyi brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
hanyi_sissi_lei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hanyi (Sissi) Lei, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Hanyi has 53 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Hanyi saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U11), where Hanyi burst onto the scene with a title in XD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Hanyi has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Hanyi promised and what Hanyi has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Hanyi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Hanyi has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Egret MW CRC, where Hanyi entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, GD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Hanyi does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Hanyi has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 4 wins means Hanyi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hanyi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Hanyi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Hanyi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hanyi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Daisy Xu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Daisy Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Enming Cao has emotionally recovered.

Of Hanyi's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Hanyi feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Hanyi has competed 6 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Then there is the seeding situation. Hanyi has been seeded in 12 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Hanyi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2026 CBA Midwest OLC in GS U17, and finishing 3rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Hanyi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Hanyi has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hanyi is not just losing locally — Hanyi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lei family travels cross-country so Hanyi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hanyi has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hanyi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15, which means Hanyi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Hanyi entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Hanyi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 53 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hanyi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hanyi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hanyi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hanyi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

hanyi_zhang.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Hanyi Zhang has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-4 in BD U15. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Hanyi belongs with the best in the country.

Hanyi is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Hanyi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-4 (BD U15) • 3 ORC titles.

  • Ethan Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Brendan Chen (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Selina Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)

With 18 seeded entries across the career, Hanyi consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 6 titles, Hanyi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
