Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Hanyi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hanyi has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hanyi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Hanyi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Hanyi entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Hanyi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 84 entries, and 78 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hanyi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hanyi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hanyi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hanyi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Hanz is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Definitely.

Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Hanz, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

hao_lin.name: Hao Lin
hao_lin.summary:
Hao Lin has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 27 entries across 11 tournaments, Hao is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Notably, Hao has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Hao is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Hao has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Hao has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Hao brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
hao_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hao Lin, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Hao has 27 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Hao saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Hao experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Hao has reached the finals 2 times — at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, 2022 SoCal Arena CRC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Hao has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hao plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Hao has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Hao's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Atticus Yong has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Hao has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Hao has been seeded in 4 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Hao takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in BS U15, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Hao. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Hao has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hao is not just losing locally — Hao is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Hao can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hao has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hao showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Hao traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Hao entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Hao paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hao keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hao has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hao, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hao. We will be here, taking notes.

haoran_xia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Haoran Xia, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 24 entries in 9 tournaments. That is a 4% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Haoran shows up to a tournament, Haoran pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Haoran saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in BD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Haoran's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Haoran actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Haoran is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Haoran experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Haoran has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Haoran does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Haoran has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Haoran is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Haoran plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Haoran and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Haoran needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Hannah Cheng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Haoran's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Haoran entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Haoran paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 24 entries, and 23 losses. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Haotian entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Haotian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 17 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Haotian. Haotian just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Haotian has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Haotian. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Haotian: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

haoyang_li.name: Haoyang Li
haoyang_li.summary:
Representing California, Haoyang Li has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 17 entries in 8 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Haoyang is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Haoyang adapts to anyone:
  • Jerry Zhengnan Mai (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #4)

Haoyang has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Haoyang brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
haoyang_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Haoyang Li, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Haoyang's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Haoyang keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Haoyang saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Haoyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Haoyang has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Jerry Zhengnan Mai (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Aaron Hong has emotionally recovered.

Haoyang has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Haoyang has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Haoyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Haoyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Haoyang entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Haoyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Haoyang keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Haoyang has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Haoyang, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Haoyang. We will be here, taking notes.

2024-2025.t: 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC

A true triple-threat, Haoyang competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Haoyang has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Raymond Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Kayla X. Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Aaron Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Kangxi Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

The 2023-2024 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Haoyang is still ascending.

Haoyang has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Haoyang isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Haoyang brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
haoyang_zheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Haoyang Zheng, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Haoyang has 28 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Haoyang saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Haoyang's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Haoyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Haoyang is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Haoyang and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Haoyang needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Haoyang has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Haoyang's texts. The go-to partner is Kayla X. Wu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Pradyun Rasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Haoyang's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Haoyang has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Haoyang has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Haoyang has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Haoyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Haoyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Haoyang entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Haoyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 28 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Haoyang looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Haoyang's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Haoyang has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Haoyang. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gowrish family travels cross-country so Hardik can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hardik has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hardik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Hardik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Hardik entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Hardik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 43 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Hardik. Hardik just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Hardik has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Hardik. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Hardik: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Haripriya has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

haripriya_tati.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Haripriya Tati, from Georgia, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Haripriya has 27 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Haripriya saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Haripriya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Haripriya has reached the finals 2 times — at 2024 South CRC (TBTT) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Haripriya has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Haripriya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Haripriya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Haripriya's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Vishanth Vijay has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Haripriya has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Haripriya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Haripriya has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Haripriya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Haripriya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Haripriya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Haripriya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 27 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Haripriya. Haripriya just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Haripriya has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Haripriya. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Haripriya: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

harrell_thomas.name: Harrell Thomas

Harrell competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Harrell has formed effective partnerships:
  • Mahith Jajula (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Harrell is still ascending.
harrell_thomas.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Harrell Thomas, from New York, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 win across 9 entries in 5 tournaments. That is a 11% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Harrell shows up to a tournament, Harrell pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Harrell saga began at 2025 NE MassBad OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2026 Wayside NE CRC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Harrell's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Harrell's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Harrell has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Harrell showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U11, which means Harrell traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Harrell entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Harrell paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 9 entries, and 8 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Harrell looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Harrell's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Harrell has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Harrell. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Harry is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Harry and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Harry needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Harry has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Harry's texts. The go-to partner is Chris Lin (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U11 — we hope Marcus Chen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Harry has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Harry has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Harry has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Harry showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U11, which means Harry traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Harry entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Harry paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 29 entries, and 26 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Harry looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Harry's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Harry has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Harry. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 17 tournaments, Harshita has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 48 entries where Harshita drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Harshita saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Harshita's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 21 entries — a stretch where Harshita actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Harshita is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Harshita experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Harshita has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Harshita does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Harshita has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Harshita is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Harshita plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Harshita is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Harshita and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Harshita needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Harshita has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Harshita's texts. The go-to partner is Thomas Wang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Thanik Bharani has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Harshita entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Harshita's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Harshita and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Harshita has competed 5 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Harshita is not just losing locally — Harshita is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kandambakkam family travels cross-country so Harshita can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Harshita has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Harshita showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19, which means Harshita traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Harshita entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Harshita paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 49 entries, and 48 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Harshita looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Harshita's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Harshita has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Harshita. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Anika Gattoju (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2)

Hasini has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Hasini brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
hasini_mateti.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hasini Mateti, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Hasini's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Hasini keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Hasini saga began at 2022 South Frisco U11 OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hasini plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Hasini has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hasini has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hasini showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11, which means Hasini traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Hasini entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Hasini paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hasini keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hasini has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hasini, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hasini. We will be here, taking notes.

haziqa_h_mathani.name: Haziqa H Mathani
haziqa_h_mathani.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Haziqa H Mathani has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Haziqa has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Haziqa competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • 1 ORC title.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Haziqa boasts 41 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a clutch 56% win rate in three-game matches (9/16), a positive +407 point differential across the career.

Haziqa has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 28 different players across the career:
  • Zisheng (GaoGao) Fan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ayaaz Muhammed Baig (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Adriel Ammy Chandran (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (6 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Chingpei Lin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Haziqa is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Haziqa has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Haziqa has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Haziqa consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Haziqa has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
haziqa_h_mathani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Haziqa H Mathani, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 79 career entries in 28 tournaments, Haziqa has 5 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Haziqa drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 79 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Haziqa saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Haziqa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Haziqa experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Haziqa has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, where Haziqa entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, GD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Haziqa does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Haziqa has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 5 wins means Haziqa is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Haziqa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Haziqa is 0-for-25. Zero titles in 25 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Haziqa and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Haziqa needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 28 of them, to be precise. Haziqa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Haziqa's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Adriel Ammy Chandran: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Adriel Ammy Chandran has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Luke Han has emotionally recovered.

Of Haziqa's 5 career titles, 4 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Haziqa feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Haziqa entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Haziqa's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Haziqa and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Haziqa has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Then there is the seeding situation. Haziqa has been seeded in 9 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Haziqa takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U13, and finishing 3rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Haziqa. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Haziqa has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Haziqa is not just losing locally — Haziqa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mathani family travels cross-country so Haziqa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Haziqa has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Haziqa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Haziqa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Haziqa entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Haziqa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 79 entries, and 74 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Haziqa looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Haziqa's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Haziqa has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Haziqa. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

henry_hongxu_zhang.name: Henry Hongxu Zhang
henry_hongxu_zhang.summary:
Representing California, Henry Hongxu Zhang has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 19 entries in 9 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Henry is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Henry adapts to anyone:
  • Evan Lu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4)
  • Aaron MX Tsai — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #5)

Henry has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Henry has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Henry's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
henry_hongxu_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Henry Hongxu Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Henry has 19 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Henry saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Henry plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Henry has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Aaron MX Tsai (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Nila Ramesh has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Henry has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Henry is not just losing locally — Henry is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Henry can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Henry has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Henry showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Henry traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Henry entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Henry paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Henry keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Henry has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Henry, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Henry. We will be here, taking notes.

Henry has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Justin Dong (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (10 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Henry has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Henry has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Henry is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
henry_tan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Henry Tan, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Henry has 64 career entries across 23 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Henry saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Henry's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Henry actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Henry is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Henry experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Henry has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Henry does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Henry has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 3 wins means Henry is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Henry plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Henry is 0-for-23. Zero titles in 23 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Henry and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Henry needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Henry has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Henry's texts. The go-to partner is Justin Dong (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Thomas Cui has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Henry's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Henry has shown up 9 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Henry has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Henry is not just losing locally — Henry is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tan family travels cross-country so Henry can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Henry has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Henry showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Henry traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Henry entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Henry paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 61 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Henry looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Henry's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Henry has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Henry. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

henry_tang.name: Henry Tang
henry_tang.summary:
Representing California, Henry Tang is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Henry in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

A true triple-threat, Henry competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

henry_wang.name: Henry Wang
henry_wang.summary:
A California competitor, Henry Wang keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U11 level, building a strong foundation across 7 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

What makes Henry especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Henry adapts to anyone:
  • Andrew Hu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Henry isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Henry's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
henry_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Henry Wang, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Henry's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Henry keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Henry saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Henry plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Henry has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Andrew Hu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U11 — we hope Andrew Hu has emotionally recovered.

Henry has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Henry has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Henry showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U11, which means Henry traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Henry entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Henry paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Henry keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Henry has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Henry, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Henry. We will be here, taking notes.
