hitansh_shah.name: Hitansh Shah
hitansh_shah.summary:
A Georgia competitor, Hitansh Shah is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 15 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Hitansh especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Hitansh has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Aashreeti Deo (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Hitansh has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Hitansh has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Hitansh's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
hitansh_shah.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hitansh Shah, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Hitansh's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Hitansh keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Hitansh saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hitansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Hitansh has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Aashreeti Deo (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Hitansh is not just losing locally — Hitansh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shah family travels cross-country so Hitansh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hitansh has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hitansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Hitansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Hitansh entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Hitansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Hitansh keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Hitansh has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Hitansh, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Hitansh. We will be here, taking notes.

Hrishikesh has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Hrishikesh's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
hrishikesh_gollamudi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hrishikesh Gollamudi, from Indiana, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Hrishikesh has 15 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Hrishikesh saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Play N Thrive MW CRC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Hrishikesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Hrishikesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Hrishikesh is 0-for-6. Zero titles in 6 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Hrishikesh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Hrishikesh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Hrishikesh has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Sahasra Samarthy (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Ellie Wen has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Hrishikesh's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Hrishikesh has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Hrishikesh has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hrishikesh has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hrishikesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Hrishikesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Hrishikesh entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Hrishikesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 15 entries, and 14 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Hrishikesh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Hrishikesh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Hrishikesh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Hrishikesh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

hrydayesh_parida.name: Hrydayesh Parida
hrydayesh_parida.summary:
Representing North Carolina, Hrydayesh Parida is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 7 entries across 4 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Hrydayesh competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Hrydayesh isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
hrydayesh_parida.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Hrydayesh Parida, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. Hrydayesh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Hrydayesh has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Hrydayesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Hrydayesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Hrydayesh is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Hrydayesh wants to be. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Hrydayesh, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

huaiyuan_ni.name: Huaiyuan Ni
huaiyuan_ni.summary:
A Texas competitor, Huaiyuan Ni has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Huaiyuan's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Huaiyuan has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Huaiyuan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Huaiyuan adapts to anyone:
  • Rishita Tubati — 2 events in XD, best: #4

Huaiyuan has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Huaiyuan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Huaiyuan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
huaiyuan_ni.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Huaiyuan Ni, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 29 career entries in 11 tournaments, Huaiyuan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 28 entries where Huaiyuan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 29 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Huaiyuan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Huaiyuan would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Huaiyuan has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Huaiyuan has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Huaiyuan's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Huaiyuan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Huaiyuan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Huaiyuan's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Geoffray Lee has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Huaiyuan entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Huaiyuan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Huaiyuan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Huaiyuan has shown up 5 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Huaiyuan has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Huaiyuan is not just losing locally — Huaiyuan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ni family travels cross-country so Huaiyuan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Huaiyuan has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Huaiyuan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U17, which means Huaiyuan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Huaiyuan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Huaiyuan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Huaiyuan. Huaiyuan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Huaiyuan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Huaiyuan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Huaiyuan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
